topics.topicName: Methods: Passing and Returning References of an Object
Methods: Passing and Returning References of an Object

skills.summary: Assess the first step of a safe three-step swap using a temporary variable.
Assess standard integer-division syntax without unnecessary casting.
Assess the assignment pattern that removes the ones place from a positive integer.
Assess the standard AP CSA rounding idiom for positive doubles.
Assess correct compound-assignment syntax for subtraction.
Assess class-level method-call syntax.
Assess standard scaling of `Math.random()` into an inclusive integer range starting at 0.
Assess parameterized object construction and storage syntax.

skills.displayName: Calling an Instance Method with No Parameters
skills.summary: Assess the standard object-method call syntax.
Assess parentheses around arithmetic inside String concatenation.
Write a boolean expression using a relational operator.
Write an if statement header with parentheses and opening brace.
Write an else if header with a condition.
Assess pure boolean boundary conditions over numeric values and generic index limits.
Assess distribution of a negation across a compound condition.
Assess whether the student can express when a `while` loop should keep running.
Assess the standard counted-loop header that runs exactly `{{intVar1}}` times.
Assess the robust odd-number condition.

skills.displayName: Safe Outer Loop Bound for Substring Search
skills.summary: Assess the boundary algebra for fixed-length substring search.
Assess execution counts in simple nested-loop structures, including rectangular, triangular, and placement-contrast forms.
Assess the standard encapsulated field declaration pattern.

skills.displayName: Constructor Signature Without a Return Type
skills.summary: Assess the syntax of a constructor header with one parameter.
Assess the header for a public method that returns a value.
Pass an array variable as an argument to a method.
Assess class-level access to a user-defined static variable.

skills.displayName: Declaring a Variable Outside a Narrower Block
skills.summary: Assess declaration in a scope wide enough for later use, with varied block contexts.
Assess the use of `this.` when a parameter name matches an instance variable name.
Assess reverse traversal syntax with an inclusive stop at index 0.

skills.displayName: Array Instantiation with an Explicit Size
skills.summary: Assess standard declaration and instantiation of an integer array.

skills.displayName: Enhanced for Loop Header for a 1D Collection
skills.summary: Assess enhanced-for headers over 1D arrays and ArrayLists.
Assess the assignment that copies the current element into the slot immediately to its left.
Create a File object for a text-file path.

skills.displayName: Autoboxing an int into an Integer
skills.summary: Use Java wrapper autoboxing in a simple assignment.
Assess the standard element-access method for ArrayLists.
Update a running total from the current ArrayList element.
Assess standard row-then-column access syntax.
Assess whether the student can choose the column count for the outer loop in a column-major traversal.
Assess boolean expressions that guard access to left, right, up, or down neighbors in a rectangular 2D array.

skills.displayName: Array Linear Search Match Flag Update
skills.summary: Set a boolean flag when a linear-search target is found.

drills.promptTemplate: You are swapping two elements in {{arrayVar}}. Write the statement that saves the value currently at index {{intVar}} into the existing temporary variable {{tempVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: This is the first step of a swap.
Assign the array element to the temporary variable.
Include a semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: You are swapping the values of {{intVar1}} and {{intVar2}}. The temporary integer tempVal has already been declared. Write the first assignment statement needed for the swap.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Store the original value of {{intVar1}} in the temporary variable first.
Do not overwrite {{intVar1}} before saving its original value.
Use = for assignment, not ==.

hintSequence: Preserve the original value of the first variable before you overwrite anything.
The temporary variable tempVal should receive the current value of {{intVar1}}.
Put tempVal on the left side of `=`.

drills.promptTemplate: You are swapping the values of {{intVar1}} and {{intVar2}}. The temporary integer tempVal has already been declared. Write the first assignment statement needed for the swap.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Store the original value of {{intVar1}} in the temporary variable first.
Do not overwrite {{intVar1}} before saving its original value.
Use = for assignment, not ==.

hintSequence: Preserve the original value of the first variable before you overwrite anything.
The temporary variable tempVal should receive the current value of {{intVar1}}.
Put tempVal on the left side of `=`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an expression to divide {{varName}} by {{divisorVar}}.
hintSequence: Identify the mathematical operation or cast needed for the expression.
Use standard arithmetic operators like +, -, *, /, or %.
Be careful to use parentheses if you need to enforce order of operations.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that divides the integer variable {{intVar1}} by the integer variable {{intVar2}} using standard integer division.

mistakePatterns.feedback: You have the dividend and divisor swapped.
Use / for division. The % operator gives the remainder.
Do not cast here. The prompt asks for standard integer division.

hintSequence: Use the standard Java division operator `/`.
Integer division in Java automatically truncates the decimal part; no cast is needed.
Apply the division operator to the two variables to perform integer division.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that divides the integer variable {{intVar1}} by the literal {{intLiteral1}} using standard integer division.

mistakePatterns.feedback: You have the dividend and divisor swapped.
Use / for division. The % operator gives the remainder.
Do not cast here. The prompt asks for standard integer division.

hintSequence: Use the standard Java division operator `/`.
Integer division in Java automatically truncates the decimal part; no cast is needed.
Apply the division operator between the variable and the literal.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the assignment statement that updates the positive integer {{intVar1}} by removing its last digit.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Modulo keeps the last digit. Integer division removes it.
This computes the value but does not update the variable. Use an assignment statement (= or /=).

hintSequence: Use integer division by 10.
The variable must be updated with the result.
Write a full assignment statement, not just an expression.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the assignment statement that updates the positive integer {{intVar1}} by removing its last digit.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Modulo keeps the last digit. Integer division removes it.
This computes the value but does not update the variable. Use an assignment statement (= or /=).

hintSequence: Use integer division by 10.
The variable must be updated with the result.
Write a full assignment statement, not just an expression.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the assignment statement that updates the positive integer {{intVar1}} by removing its last digit.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Modulo keeps the last digit. Integer division removes it.
This computes the value but does not update the variable. Use an assignment statement (= or /=).

hintSequence: Use integer division by 10.
The variable must be updated with the result.
Write a full assignment statement, not just an expression.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an expression that rounds the double {{doubleVar}} to the nearest integer using casting, after adding 0.5.
hintSequence: Identify the mathematical operation or cast needed for the expression.
Use standard arithmetic operators like +, -, *, /, or %.
Be careful to use parentheses if you need to enforce order of operations.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the standard AP-style expression that rounds the positive double variable {{doubleVar}} to the nearest integer.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Add 0.5 before the cast, not after it. Use parentheses around the whole addition.
Use the standard AP casting pattern for this drill.

hintSequence: In AP CSA, you often round a positive double by adding 0.5 and then casting to an `int`.
The addition must happen BEFORE the cast. Use parentheses to group the addition.
Add `0.5` before casting to `int`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the standard AP-style expression that rounds the positive double variable {{doubleVar}} to the nearest integer.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Add 0.5 before the cast, not after it. Use parentheses around the whole addition.
Use the standard AP casting pattern for this drill.

hintSequence: In AP CSA, you often round a positive double by adding 0.5 and then casting to an `int`.
The addition must happen BEFORE the cast. Use parentheses to group the addition.
Add `0.5` before casting to `int`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement to multiply the existing double variable {{varName}} by {{doubleLiteral}} using compound assignment.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use the compound multiplication assignment operator '*='.
Assume{{varName}}is already declared; just perform the assignment.

hintSequence: You need to update a variable based on its current value.
Use compound assignment (like += or -=) or increment operators (++ or --).
Write the variable name followed by the operator to update it compactly.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that decrements the integer variable {{intVar}} by 1 using the postfix operator.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use the postfix operator (after the variable), not the prefix operator.
The prompt specifies using the decrement operator, not subtraction assignment.

hintSequence: The postfix operator is placed after the variable.
The decrement operator decreases the value by 1.
Write the variable followed by `--`.

drills.promptTemplate: Use a compound assignment operator to subtract {{intVar2}} from {{intVar1}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use -= for compound subtraction. =- assigns the negative value instead.
This is valid subtraction, but the prompt specifically requires a compound assignment operator.

hintSequence: Put the arithmetic operator before the equals sign.
The left side is still {{intVar1}}.
Use the combined operator for subtraction and assignment.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that calls the static method `resetAll()` from the class {{className}}. The method takes no parameters.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: External static calls require the class name followed by a dot.
The pattern is ClassName.methodName().
Use the class name, a dot, and the static method call.

drills.promptTemplate: Assuming you are inside the class {{className}}, write the statement that calls its own static method {{methodName}} which takes no arguments.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: Internal static calls do not require the class name prefix.
Just write the method name followed by parentheses.
Don't forget the semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: Write a statement to call the static method {{staticMethodName}} from the {{className}} class, which returns a primitive integer, with no arguments, saving the result into the newly-declared variable {{intVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: You call static methods using the Class name directly (ClassName.method()), not an object instance.

hintSequence: Static members belong to the class itself rather than individual objects.
To access a static method or variable, call it on the class name directly.
Write the ClassName, a dot, and the member name.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that generates a random integer from 0 to `{{intVar1}}`, inclusive.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The method name israndom, lowercase.
Use the staticMath.random()method.

hintSequence: Start with Math.random() to get a value from 0.0 up to but not including 1.0.
Scale by the inclusive maximum plus one.
Cast to int to truncate to a whole number.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that generates a random integer from `0` up to and including {{intVar1}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This generates values from 0 to {{intVar1}}-1. Add 1 to include {{intVar1}}.
Casting Math.random() directly to (int) always results in 0. Cast the result of the multiplication instead.

hintSequence: Multiply `Math.random()` by the number of possible outcomes.
To include {{intVar1}}, you need `{{intVar1}} + 1` possible integers.
Don't forget to cast the entire result to an `(int)`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that generates a random integer from `0` up to and including {{intVar1}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This generates values from 0 to {{intVar1}}-1. Add 1 to include {{intVar1}}.
Casting Math.random() directly to (int) always results in 0. Cast the result of the multiplication instead.

hintSequence: Multiply `Math.random()` by the number of possible outcomes.
To include {{intVar1}}, you need `{{intVar1}} + 1` possible integers.
Don't forget to cast the entire result to an `(int)`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that declares {{objectVar}} as type {{className}} and initializes it by passing the String "{{stringLiteral}}" and the integer {{intLiteral}} to the constructor.
hintSequence: Pass the arguments in the same order they are requested in the description.
The syntax is `new ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...);`.

drills.promptTemplate: The variable {{objectVar}} already refers to a {{className}} object. Write the assignment statement that replaces it with a new object created by passing the integers {{intLiteral1}} and {{intLiteral2}} to the constructor.
hintSequence: Pass the arguments in the same order they are requested in the description.
The syntax is `new ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...);`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that declares {{objectVar}} as type {{className}} and initializes it by passing the String "{{stringLiteral}}" and the double {{doubleLiteral}} to the constructor.
hintSequence: Pass the arguments in the same order they are requested in the description.
The syntax is `new ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...);`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that calls the method `startEngine()` on the object {{myCar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Call the method using parentheses (). Without parentheses, you are targeting the method reference, not executing it.

hintSequence: Use dot notation (`.`) to access methods on an object.
A method call always requires parentheses `()`, even if there are no parameters.
Place the method name after the dot and include empty parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that calls the method `{{methodName}}()` on the object {{objectVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Call the method using parentheses (). Without parentheses, you are targeting the method reference, not executing it.

hintSequence: Use dot notation (`.`) to access methods on an object.
A method call always requires parentheses `()`, even if there are no parameters.
Place the method name after the dot and include empty parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that calls the method `nextLine()` on the object `scanner` to read the next line.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Call the method using parentheses (). Without parentheses, you are targeting the method reference, not executing it.

hintSequence: Use dot notation (`.`) to access methods on an object.
A method call always requires parentheses `()`, even if there are no parameters.
Place the method name after the dot and include empty parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that declares a String variable named {{stringVar}} and initializes it dynamically to the literal string ""{{strLit}}" " followed directly by the mathematical sum of {{intVar1}} and {{intVar2}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Group the arithmetic so the integers add before String concatenation continues.

hintSequence: Once a String operates via the + symbol, following additions act as text concatenation.
Group the arithmetic operation in parentheses to bypass strictly left-to-right type coercion.
Put the arithmetic addition in parentheses before concatenating.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that declares a String variable named {{stringVar}} whose text holds the mathematical sum of {{intVar1}} and {{intVar2}} followed by the text " "{{strLit}}"".

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: The mathematical sum evaluates naturally from left to right prior to hitting the string.
To be defensively secure, enclose the mathematical addition operation inside grouping parentheses.
Put the arithmetic addition in parentheses before the trailing string.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that concatenates the string variable {{stringVar1}} with the sum of the integer variables {{intVar1}} and {{intVar2}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Without parentheses, Java concatenates the numbers one by one as strings instead of adding them first.

hintSequence: In Java, string concatenation (+) happens from left to right.
To add the integers before concatenating them with the string, you must use parentheses.
Use parentheses so the integer sum happens before concatenation.

drills.promptTemplate: Write a boolean expression that checks whether {{score}} is less than {{limit}}.
hintSequence: A relational operator compares two numeric values and yields true or false.
Use the operator named in the prompt, then place the variables on either side of it.

drills.promptTemplate: Write a boolean expression evaluating to true if {{intVarA}} is less than or equal to {{intVarB}}.
hintSequence: Relational expressions evaluate to true or false by comparing values.
Use operators like >, <, ==, or != to form your condition.
Combine multiple conditions using logical AND (&&) or OR (||).

drills.promptTemplate: Write an if statement header that executes when {{minVar}} is less than {{numVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Using '>' is wrong direction for min check; use '<'.
Equality check, not comparison.

hintSequence: Relational expressions evaluate to true or false by comparing values.
Use operators like >, <, ==, or != to form your condition.
Combine multiple conditions using logical AND (&&) or OR (||).

drills.promptTemplate: Write just the header for an if statement that executes only if the boolean variable {{boolVar}} is false.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use '==' for comparison. If checking for false, '!var' or 'var == false' are common.
The prompt asks only for the header, not the opening brace '{'.

hintSequence: Use the `if` keyword with a boolean condition in parentheses.
To test for false, negate the variable or compare it to `false`.
Do not include braces or a semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for an `if` statement that checks whether the integer {{intVar}} is negative.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: Use the if keyword followed by parentheses.
A number is negative if it is less than 0.
Do not include any braces or semi-colons.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an `if` statement that checks if the boolean flag {{booleanVar}} is true and, if so, further checks if the integer {{intVar}} is positive. Use a nested if structure on one line WITHOUT using logical AND (&&) in the conditions.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The prompt specifies using nested if statements instead of the && operator.

hintSequence: Write the first if checking the boolean flag.
Immediately follow it with a second if checking the numeric variable.
Do not use any curly braces or && operators.

drills.promptTemplate: In a multi-way conditional, write the header for the `else if` block that checks whether {{intVar}} is exactly equal to 0.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use else if for a middle condition in a chain.

hintSequence: Use the keywords else if.
Use the double equals (==) operator for the check.
Do not include any braces or semi-colons.

drills.promptTemplate: Write just the header for an else if statement checking if integer {{varName}} is less than zero.

mistakePatterns.feedback: else ifis two words in Java.
Anelse ifheader doesn't end with a semicolon.

hintSequence: Use the `else if` keyword sequence.
Compare the integer to zero with `<`.
Do not include braces or a semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the `else if` header that handles {{condition}}.
hintSequence: The else-if header includes the keywords, parentheses, and the opening brace.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the boolean expression that checks whether {{count}} is at least {{minCount}} and at most {{maxCount}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Both endpoints are included in the interval.

hintSequence: At least the minimum means the lower bound is inclusive.
At most the maximum means the upper bound is inclusive.
Use && to combine the two comparisons.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an expression that evaluates to true if {{intVar}} is outside the inclusive range {{intLow}} to {{intHigh}}.
hintSequence: Outside the range means the value is either below the lower bound or above the upper bound.
Use < for the lower side and >= for the upper side.
Join the two outside checks with ||.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that is true when {{age}} is at least 13 and less than 19.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The upper bound is exclusive.

hintSequence: At least 13 means the lower bound is inclusive.
Less than 19 means the upper bound is exclusive.
Use && to join the two comparisons.

drills.promptTemplate: According to De Morgan's Law, rewrite the expression `!({{booleanVar1}} && {{booleanVar2}})` without the outer negation and without outer parentheses.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Flip the logical AND (&&) to a logical OR (||).

hintSequence: Apply the negation to each internal term.
Flip the logical AND (&&) to its opposite.
Negate each term and change `&&` to `||`.

drills.promptTemplate: Rewrite the expression `!(!{{booleanVar1}} && {{intVar1}} < 10)` without the outer negation and without outer parentheses.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Ensure the operator is flipped and internal negations are resolved.

hintSequence: Negate !{{booleanVar1}} to get {{booleanVar1}}.
Negate {{intVar1}} < 10 to get {{intVar1}} >= 10.
Flip the operator to ||.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an expression that evaluates to false when at least one of {{varA}} or {{varB}} is true, using a single not operator.
hintSequence: You need to invert a logical expression.
Remember that applying NOT (!) to an AND (&&) expression converts it to OR (||).
Distribute the negation through the expression to flip the conditions.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the simplest `while` loop header that runs as long as the boolean flag {{booleanVar}} is true.

mistakePatterns.feedback: A boolean variable is already a condition. No need to compare to true.

hintSequence: You can use a boolean variable directly as a condition.
Do not use == true.
Use the `while` keyword followed by the boolean variable in parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the `while` loop header that runs as long as the integer {{intVar}} is less than 10 AND the boolean variable {{booleanVar}} is false.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use && to combine both required conditions.

hintSequence: The first part is {{intVar}} < 10.
The second part is !{{booleanVar}}.
Combine them using the logical AND (&&) operator.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the `while` loop condition that keeps the loop running as long as {{intVar1}} is strictly less than {{intVar2}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: That is the exit condition. Write the continuation condition.

hintSequence: The while condition should stay true while work continues.
Use the less-than operator.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for a for loop where {{loopVar}} starts at {{firstVar}} and increments by 1 while less than {{limitVar}}*2.

mistakePatterns.feedback: <= includes limitVar; prompt expects strictly less than limitVar * 2.
Expected pre-increment syntax; use ++ before the loop variable.

hintSequence: A standard for-loop header requires three distinct parts separated by semicolons.
Provide the initialization, the continuation condition, and the step update.
Ensure your loop condition prevents out-of-bounds access if traversing an array.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for a `for` loop that uses index variable {{intVar}}, starts at index 1, and goes up to (but not including) the length of the array {{arrayVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: Set the initial index to 1.
Use the standard length-based stopping condition.
Increment by 1 each time.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for an indexed for loop that iterates through all valid indices of an integer array named {{arrName}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Check condition: Using '<=' with array.length often goes out of bounds.
This looks like a for-each loop. Use an indexed loop (int i = 0; ...).

hintSequence: A standard for-loop header requires three distinct parts separated by semicolons.
Provide the initialization, the continuation condition, and the step update.
Ensure your loop condition prevents out-of-bounds access if traversing an array.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the boolean expression that is true when the integer variable {{intVar1}} is odd. The value may be negative.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use != 0 so the condition also works for negative odd numbers. In Java, -3 % 2 is -1.
Use modulo (%) to check parity, not division (/).

hintSequence: Odd numbers are not evenly divisible by 2.
Check the remainder when dividing by 2 using the `%` operator.
The safest condition that works for negative numbers is checking if the remainder is not 0.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the boolean expression that is true when the integer variable {{intVar1}} is odd. The value may be negative.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use != 0 so the condition also works for negative odd numbers. In Java, -3 % 2 is -1.
Use modulo (%) to check parity, not division (/).

hintSequence: Odd numbers are not evenly divisible by 2.
Check the remainder when dividing by 2 using the `%` operator.
The safest condition that works for negative numbers is checking if the remainder is not 0.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the boolean expression that is true when the integer variable {{intVar1}} is odd. The value may be negative.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use != 0 so the condition also works for negative odd numbers. In Java, -3 % 2 is -1.
Use modulo (%) to check parity, not division (/).

hintSequence: Odd numbers are not evenly divisible by 2.
Check the remainder when dividing by 2 using the `%` operator.
The safest condition that works for negative numbers is checking if the remainder is not 0.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the `for` loop header that uses loop variable {{intVar1}} and iterates high enough to check all substrings of {{stringVar1}} with the same length as {{stringVar2}} safely.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Running to the very end will cause an IndexOutOfBoundsException if extracting substrings.

hintSequence: Stop the loop before the window falls off the end of the string.
Use <= with length - length.

drills.promptTemplate: Write a `for` loop header starting at 0 that uses `{{intVar1}}` to safely stop before the window of length `{{stringVar2}}.length()` goes out of bounds when searching `{{stringVar1}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Running to the very end will cause an IndexOutOfBoundsException if extracting substrings.

hintSequence: The loop counter represents the start index of each substring.
Subtract the substring length from the total length to find the limit.

drills.promptTemplate: Construct the `for` loop statement with loop variable `{{intVar1}}` needed to safely examine each substring of `{{stringVar1}}` whose length matches `{{stringVar2}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Running to the very end will cause an IndexOutOfBoundsException if extracting substrings.

hintSequence: Substrings are extracted using a starting and ending index.
Your loop variable should only go up to `source.length() - target.length()`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression representing how many times the inner-body statement executes when the outer loop runs from 1 through {{intVar1}} and the inner loop runs from 1 through the current outer value.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Multiply the outer and inner counts to find the total iterations.

hintSequence: The total is the sum of the repeated inner counts across each outer iteration.
The pattern grows like 1 + 2 + ... + n.
Use the triangular-number formula when the prompt describes that shape.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression representing how many times the inner-body statement executes when the outer loop runs {{intVar1}} times and the inner loop runs {{intVar2}} times.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Multiply the nested counts, do not add them.

hintSequence: For each outer iteration, the full inner loop runs again.
Combine repeated groups with multiplication.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression representing how many times the statement in the outer loop body executes if the outer loop runs `{{intVar1}}` times and that statement appears both before and after the inner loop inside each outer iteration.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Multiply the nested counts, do not add them.

hintSequence: Count the outer-loop passes first.
The statement happens twice during each outer pass.
Multiply the outer-loop count by 2.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement to declare a private instance variable named {{arrVar}} that holds an array of int values.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Don't forget the square brackets to indicate it is an array.

hintSequence: You need the private access modifier, the data type representation of an int array, and the variable name.
An array of ints is represented with square brackets after the primitive type.
Write private, then int[], followed by the variable name and a semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement to declare a private instance variable named {{objVar}} of the custom type {{className}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Write the visibility modifier explicitly.

hintSequence: Instance variables should use the appropriate access modifier for encapsulation.
The type of the variable is the name of the custom class.
Write private {{className}} {{objVar}} followed by a semicolon.

drills.promptTemplate: Declare a private instance variable named `items` of type `ArrayList<String>`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Instance variables should be private to follow encapsulation principles.

hintSequence: Use the `private` keyword to restrict access to the class itself.
Instance variables are usually declared at the top of the class block.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside the {{className}} class, write the public constructor header that takes an array of double values named {{arrParam}} as its only argument. Do not include braces.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The parameter type should be written as double[].
Constructors do not define return types.

hintSequence: Use the class name for the constructor and do not include a return type.
Declare the array argument using square brackets after the primitive type.
Write public {{className}}, followed by the double[] type and the parameter name in parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside the {{className}} class, write the public constructor header that takes a String parameter named {{strParam}} followed by an int parameter named {{intParam}}. Do not include braces.

mistakePatterns.feedback: A constructor does not specify a return type.
Make sure to include the parameter types along with their names.

hintSequence: The constructor header specifies the access modifier, class name, and parameter list.
A constructor never has a return type.
Put the String parameter first and the int parameter second, separated by a comma.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the public constructor header for a class named `Game` that takes parameters: `none`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Constructors MUST NOT have a return type, not even void.

hintSequence: Constructors do not have a return type.
Constructors must have the exact same name as the class.
Do not include `void` or any other type in the header.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the public method header for a method named `getName` that returns a `String`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This method must return a value, so the return type cannot be void.

hintSequence: A method header includes the access modifier, return type, name, and parameters.
The syntax is `public ReturnType methodName(Parameters)`.
For methods that return a value, the return type must not be `void`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the public method header for a method named `calculateTotal` that returns a `double` and takes parameters: `int quantity`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This method must return a value, so the return type cannot be void.

hintSequence: A method header includes the access modifier, return type, name, and parameters.
The syntax is `public ReturnType methodName(Parameters)`.
For methods that return a value, the return type must not be `void`.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the complete header for the standard toString method inherited from Object.

mistakePatterns.feedback: toStringmust return aString.
Method name is toString, which is camelCase with capital S.

hintSequence: Method headers specify visibility, return type, name, and parameters.
Accessors (getters) should return the appropriate instance variable type.
Start with public, then the return type, the method name, and parentheses.

drills.promptTemplate: Suppose there is a method `calculate(int[] arr)`. Write the statement that calls this method using the existing array variable {{arrayVar}} as the argument.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Matches are not part of a method call argument.

hintSequence: Pass the array variable name inside the method parentheses.
Do not use brackets when passing the variable itself as an argument.
Call the method with the array variable as the argument.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for a public method named `processData` that has a single parameter which is an integer array named `data`. The method returns nothing.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: The return type is void.
The parameter type is int[].
Make the parameter an `int[]` in the method header.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the method call that supplies the array {{arrayVar}} to {{methodName}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Do not leave the argument list empty.
Do not wrap the array argument in square brackets.

hintSequence: The array variable should appear as the method argument.
Do not add brackets around the argument.

drills.promptTemplate: How would you access the public static constant {{constantName}} from the {{className}} class?

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: Use the class name and dot notation.
Static members are accessed directly on the class.
Use the class name followed by a dot and then the member name.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that accesses the static variable {{constantName1}} from the {{className1}} class.
hintSequence: Use the class name, a dot, and the member name.

drills.promptTemplate: Refer to the shared variable {{constantName9}} defined in the {{className9}} class.
hintSequence: Use the class name to access its shared (static) variables.
Dot notation is used for access.
Put the class qualifier before the shared member name.

drills.promptTemplate: You need the int variable {{intVar1}} to remain available after an `if` statement finishes. Write the declaration statement that should appear before the `if` statement, initializing it to 0.
hintSequence: A variable only exists within the block where it is declared.
Declare it in the wider scope first.

drills.promptTemplate: You need the double variable {{doubleVar1}} to remain available after a `while` statement finishes. Write the declaration statement that should appear before the `while` statement, initializing it to 0.0.
hintSequence: A variable only exists within the block where it is declared.
Declare it in the wider scope first.

drills.promptTemplate: You need the String variable {{stringVar1}} to remain available after a nested block finishes. Write the declaration statement that should appear before the nested block, initializing it to "".
hintSequence: A variable only exists within the block where it is declared.
Declare it in the wider scope first.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the assignment statement needed within a constructor or setter to assign the value of a parameter {{paramName}} to an instance variable with the same name.

mistakePatterns.feedback: You need to usethis.to distinguish the instance variable from the parameter.
This is an assignment (=), not a comparison (==).

hintSequence: The this keyword refers to the current object instance.
Use this.fieldName to differentiate an instance variable from a parameter with the same name.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the assignment statement needed within a constructor or setter to assign the value of a double parameter {{paramName}} times {{multiplier}} into an instance variable with the same name as the parameter.

mistakePatterns.feedback: You need to usethis.to distinguish the instance variable from the parameter.
This is an assignment (=), not a comparison (==).

hintSequence: The this keyword refers to the current object instance.
Use this.fieldName to differentiate an instance variable from a parameter with the same name.

drills.promptTemplate: When using constructor chaining, at which line position must the `this(...)` call be placed?

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: Think about Java order of operations for object setup.
Constructor chaining must happen before any other code in the body.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for a for loop that iterates backwards through all valid indices of array {{arrName}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Iterating backwards usually involves decrementing (--).
Check the loop condition; iterating backwards usually involves>= 0.
Start index is usuallylength - 1.

hintSequence: Start at the last valid index, which is length - 1.
Continue while the index is greater than or equal to 0.
Decrement the loop variable each iteration.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the complete header for a `for` loop that traverses the array {{arrayVar}} from the last element down to index 0, inclusive, using loop variable {{intVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Arrays use .length, not .length() method.

hintSequence: The last valid index is {{arrayVar}}.length - 1.
The loop should stop when it passes 0, so the condition is >= 0.
Decrement the index each time.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the header for a `for` loop that starts {{intVar1}} at {{intVar2}} and counts down to 0, inclusive.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use >= 0 to include the 0 index.

hintSequence: Initialize at the starting value.
Use >= 0 to include zero.
Decrement the variable.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement to declare a String array {{arrName}} capable of holding {{intLiteral}} elements, without initializing the elements.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This asks for declaration with a size usingnew, not an initializer list using{}.
Use square brackets[]for array size, not parentheses().

hintSequence: This drill uses `new` with a size, not an initializer list.
Put the size inside square brackets after the element type.
The declaration should not include curly braces.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that declares the variable {{arrayVar}} as an integer array and allocates it with a size of 10.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Specify the size inside the brackets of the new allocation.

hintSequence: The type is int[].
Use the `new` keyword and specify the exact size inside square brackets.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the declaration that creates a 2D String array named {{arrName}} with first row "{{strLitA}}", "{{strLitB}}" and second row "{{strLitC}}", "{{strLitD}}".

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use a nested initializer list directly here.
Use braces for the nested initializer list.
Keep the rows in separate nested brace groups.

hintSequence: This drill uses a nested initializer list, not `new`.
Put each row in its own pair of curly braces.
Separate the row groups with commas.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the enhanced `for` loop header that traverses the integer array {{arrayVar}} using {{intVar1}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use a colon (:), not a semicolon, in an enhanced for loop.

hintSequence: Enhanced for loops use a colon (:).
Declare the element type (int).

drills.promptTemplate: Write the enhanced `for` loop header that traverses the String array {{arrayVar}} using {{stringVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use a colon (:), not a semicolon, in an enhanced for loop.

hintSequence: Enhanced for loops use a colon (:).
Declare the element type (String).

drills.promptTemplate: Write the enhanced `for` loop header that traverses the ArrayList<Student> variable roster using a local variable named s.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Match the required syntax.

hintSequence: The element type is Student.
The local variable name is s.
The collection name is roster.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a loop with index {{indexVar}}, write the statement that moves the current element into the slot immediately to its left in the array {{arrayVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: That shifts from left to right. Reverse the indices for a left shift.

hintSequence: The destination slot is at index `{{indexVar}} - 1`.
The source value is at index `{{indexVar}}`.
Assignment moves from the source (right) to the destination (left).

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a loop, write the statement that performs the "left shift" assignment for the current index {{indexVar}} in the array {{arrayVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: That shifts from left to right. Reverse the indices for a left shift.

hintSequence: The destination slot is at index `{{indexVar}} - 1`.
The source value is at index `{{indexVar}}`.
Assignment moves from the source (right) to the destination (left).

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a loop, write the statement that shifts the value from {{indexVar}} to {{indexVar}} - 1 in the array {{arrayVar}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: That shifts from left to right. Reverse the indices for a left shift.

hintSequence: The destination slot is at index `{{indexVar}} - 1`.
The source value is at index `{{indexVar}}`.
Assignment moves from the source (right) to the destination (left).

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that creates a File object for the text-file path `{{pathVar}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This prompt asks for the File object, not the Scanner.
Use the constructor call with new.

hintSequence: The path string belongs inside the File constructor.
Use `new File(...)`.
Do not add a Scanner yet.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the constructor call to create a `File` instance from the string `{{stringVar}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The constructor call should target the File class.
Remember the "new" keyword for object creation.

hintSequence: Use `new File`.
Place `{{stringVar}}` inside the parentheses.
This represents a file on disk that a Scanner can read.

drills.promptTemplate: To begin reading from a text file located at `{{pathVar}}`, you must first instantiate a `File` object. Write the expression that does this.

mistakePatterns.feedback: This prompt asks for the File object, not the Scanner.
Use the constructor call with new.

hintSequence: The `File` constructor takes the path String as an argument.
Use `new` followed by the class name.
Do not create a Scanner yet; just create the File.

drills.promptTemplate: Declare an Integer object {{intObj}} and initialize it with the primitive int value {{intVar}} using autoboxing.
hintSequence: This is autoboxing, so the primitive int should be assigned to an Integer.
Use the wrapper class name `Integer`.
Do not call parse or value-conversion methods here.

drills.promptTemplate: Assign the int variable {{intVar1}} to the existing Integer wrapper variable {{instanceVar1}}. Use autoboxing.

mistakePatterns.feedback: While using a constructor works, it is deprecated in favor of autoboxing or valueOf(). Use direct assignment for autoboxing.

hintSequence: Java automatically converts primitives to their wrapper types (autoboxing).
You can perform a direct assignment without using `new` or constructor calls.
Assign the primitive value directly to the wrapper variable to trigger autoboxing.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the statement that stores the primitive int `{{intVar}}` in the Integer object `{{integerObj}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The left-hand variable should be an Integer object, not a primitive int.
Use direct assignment for autoboxing, not a constructor call.

hintSequence: The wrapper type is Integer.
Direct assignment performs autoboxing.
Declare the object variable with the Integer type.

drills.promptTemplate: Retrieve the element at index `{{indexVar}}` from the `ArrayList<String>` `{{listName}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use the.get(index)method for ArrayLists, not array bracket[]notation.

hintSequence: ArrayLists require method calls to access or modify their elements.
Use the .get() method to read a value and .set() to update one.
Write the list name followed by the appropriate method call and arguments.

drills.promptTemplate: Read the element at the very end of `the ArrayList {{listName}}` using its index.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use .get(index) for ArrayLists. Square brackets [] are only for arrays.
Use parentheses () with the .get method, not square brackets.

hintSequence: Calculate the index of the last element using the `.size()` method.
The last index is always one less than the current size of the list.
Pass this calculated value as the argument to the access method.

drills.promptTemplate: Retrieve the first element (index 0) from `the ArrayList {{listName}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use .get(index) for ArrayLists. Square brackets [] are only for arrays.
Use parentheses () with the .get method, not square brackets.

hintSequence: The first element in any zero-indexed structure is at index 0.
Recall that the first valid index in Java is always 0.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a loop, write the statement that adds the current element of `the ArrayList {{listVar}}` at index `{{indexVar}}` to the running total `{{sumVar}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Add the current list element to the running total instead of overwriting it.
The running total belongs on the left side of the update.

hintSequence: A running total combines the old total with the current list element.
Retrieve the list element with get(...).
Use `+=` to add the current element into the total.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the running-total update that adds `the ArrayList {{arrayListVar}}.get({{intVar1}})` into `{{sumVar}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Add the current element to the running total instead of overwriting it.
The running total belongs on the left side of the update.

hintSequence: A running total combines the old total with the current list element.
The ArrayList access stays on the right side of the update.
Use `+=` to add the current element into the total.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a loop, write the line of code that adds the value at index `{{intVar1}}` of `the ArrayList {{arrayListVar}}` to a running sum `{{sumVar}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The += operator is the standard way to update a running sum.
Use .get() to access elements from an ArrayList.

hintSequence: The target variable for the addition is {{sumVar}}.
Retrieve the current element using the get() method on the ArrayList.
Use the compound assignment operator to update the total with the retrieved element.

drills.promptTemplate: Write an expression for the element at row {{rowVar}} and column {{colVar}} of 2D array {{arrName}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use separate brackets for row and column.
Use square brackets for 2D array access.
A 2D array access needs both a row and a column index.
Use row first and column second.

hintSequence: Accessing a cell in a 2D array requires two sets of square brackets.
The first bracket always specifies the row, and the second specifies the column.

drills.promptTemplate: Write a boolean expression that is true when {{arrName}}[{{rowVar}}][{{colVar}}] equals {{intLit}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use separate brackets for row and column.
Use square brackets for 2D array access.
A 2D array access needs both a row and a column index.
Use row first and column second.
Use == when comparing an int cell to an int literal.

hintSequence: Accessing a cell in a 2D array requires two sets of square brackets.
The first bracket always specifies the row, and the second specifies the column.
Write the array name followed by [row][col].

drills.promptTemplate: Write the expression that looks up the cell at row {{rowVar}} and column {{colVar}} in {{arrName}}.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use separate brackets for row and column.
Use square brackets for 2D array access.
A 2D array access needs both a row and a column index.
Use row first and column second.

hintSequence: Accessing a cell in a 2D array requires two sets of square brackets.
The first bracket always specifies the row, and the second specifies the column.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the outer `for` loop header for a column-major traversal of the 2D array {{array2DVar1}}, using loop variable {{intVar1}} starting at 0.
hintSequence: In column-major traversal, the outer loop tracks columns.
Use the column count (the length of any row in a rectangular array) as the upper bound for the loop.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the outer `for` loop header for a column-major traversal of the 2D array {{array2DVar10}}, using loop variable {{intVar10}} starting at 0.
hintSequence: In column-major traversal, the outer loop tracks columns.
Use the column count (the length of any row in a rectangular array) as the upper bound for the loop.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the outer `for` loop header for a column-major traversal of the 2D array {{array2DVar9}}, using loop variable {{intVar9}} starting at 0.
hintSequence: In column-major traversal, the outer loop tracks columns.
Use the column count (the length of any row in a rectangular array) as the upper bound for the loop.

drills.promptTemplate: You are currently at column {{colVar1}} in the rectangular 2D array {{array2DVar1}}. Write the boolean expression that checks whether a cell to the right exists.
hintSequence: Compare the current column index to the total number of columns available in a row.
Use the length of the first row (row 0) to determine the total column count.

drills.promptTemplate: You are currently at column {{colVar9}} in the rectangular 2D array {{array2DVar9}}. Write the boolean expression that checks whether a cell to the left exists.
hintSequence: A left neighbor exists only if the current column is not the first column.
The first valid column index is 0.

drills.promptTemplate: You are currently at row {{rowVar10}} in the rectangular 2D array {{array2DVar10}}. Write the boolean expression that checks whether a cell below exists.
hintSequence: A downward neighbor exists only if the current row is not the last row.
Use the number of rows to find the last valid row index.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a linear search loop, write the `if` statement that sets `{{flagVar}}` to true when the current element of `the array {{arrayVar}}` at index `{{indexVar}}` equals the target value `{{targetValue}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: A match should set the flag to true.
Use comparison in the condition, not assignment.

hintSequence: A successful match should flip the flag on.
Compare the current array value to the target value.
Set the boolean flag to true inside the if block.

drills.promptTemplate: Inside a search loop, write the logic to flag a match by setting `{{flagVar}}` to true if the array `{{arrayVar}}[{{intVar1}}]` matches the target `{{intVar2}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: The flag update should happen inside the conditional block.
Use brackets to index into the array.

hintSequence: The logic requires an if-statement to detect the match.
The condition should compare the array element at the current index to {{intVar2}}.
Inside the block, perform the assignment {{flagVar}} = true;.

drills.promptTemplate: Write the conditional statement that updates `{{flagVar}}` to true whenever the value in `the array {{arrayVar}}` at position `{{intVar1}}` is equivalent to `{{intVar2}}`.

mistakePatterns.feedback: Use the assignment operator = to update the flag.
The conditional checks for equivalence with the target value.

hintSequence: Start with an if-block to check the condition.
Compare the value at index {{intVar1}} using the == operator.
If they are equivalent, update {{flagVar}} to true.
